label: LC-39B — SLS Launch Pad
sub: Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center is the primary launch pad for the Artemis program, rebuilt from Apollo/Shuttle-era infrastructure. All Artemis SLS/Orion missions launch from this pad.

label: Vehicle Assembly Building (VAB)
sub: The iconic VAB at KSC is where SLS and Orion are stacked and integrated before rollout to the launch pad. At 160 meters tall, it remains one of the largest buildings in the world by volume.

label: LC-36 — Blue Origin New Glenn
sub: Launch Complex 36 at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station is the dedicated launch pad for Blue Origin's New Glenn heavy-lift rocket, which first flew January 16, 2025. New Glenn supports Blue Origin's Blue Moon HLS development.

label: Johnson Space Center (JSC)
sub: JSC in Houston serves as Mission Control for all Artemis crewed flights and is the home of NASA's Astronaut Corps. The Artemis II crew — Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen — trained extensively at JSC's facilities.

label: Goddard Space Flight Center
sub: NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, MD manages Artemis-era science instruments and the Deep Space Network coordination. Goddard also oversees key Orion communications systems.

label: Marshall Space Flight Center
sub: NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, AL is the lead center for SLS development and manages the Space Launch System program office. Marshall oversees the RS-25 engine procurement contract with Aerojet Rocketdyne.

label: Stennis Space Center
sub: NASA's Stennis Space Center in Mississippi hosts the B-2 test stand where SLS underwent the Green Run engine tests in January and March 2021. Stennis is the primary facility for large rocket propulsion testing.

label: Michoud Assembly Facility (Boeing SLS)
sub: NASA's Michoud Assembly Facility in New Orleans is where Boeing manufactures the SLS core stage. The facility has manufactured rocket components since the Saturn V era. SLS core stages are built and shipped to Stennis for testing, then to KSC.

label: Lockheed Martin — Orion Prime (Colorado)
sub: Lockheed Martin's facility at Waterton Canyon, Colorado is the prime contractor headquarters for the Orion crew module. Final assembly and systems integration are performed at KSC, but Lockheed Martin leads all design, manufacturing, and testing.

label: Aerojet Rocketdyne — RS-25 Engines (Sacramento)
sub: Aerojet Rocketdyne's facility in Sacramento, CA manufactures and tests the RS-25 engines that power the SLS core stage. Sixteen heritage RS-25 engines from the Space Shuttle program were refurbished for Artemis I–IV; new production engines are being manufactured for subsequent flights.

label: Northrop Grumman — Gateway HALO (Redondo Beach)
sub: Northrop Grumman's facility in Redondo Beach, CA is building the Gateway HALO (Habitation and Logistics Outpost) module under an $935 million contract. Northrop Grumman also builds SLS solid rocket boosters with facilities in Utah and Florida.

label: SpaceX Headquarters (Hawthorne, CA)
sub: SpaceX's headquarters in Hawthorne, CA is where Starship HLS engineering and mission planning are coordinated. SpaceX holds the primary HLS contract ($2.89B) for Artemis III and beyond, developing a crewed lunar-capable Starship variant.

label: Starbase — SpaceX Starship (Boca Chica)
sub: SpaceX Starbase in Boca Chica, TX is the development and launch site for Starship/Super Heavy, the vehicle selected as the Artemis Human Landing System. Integrated flight tests, including the first orbital trajectory (IFT-3, March 2024), have been conducted here.

label: Blue Origin HQ — Blue Moon HLS (Kent, WA)
sub: Blue Origin's headquarters and primary manufacturing facility in Kent, WA is developing the Blue Moon Mk.2 crewed lunar lander under a $3.4B NASA contract. New Shepard and New Glenn propulsion and vehicle systems are also developed here.

label: ULA — Centaur V Manufacturing (Decatur, AL)
sub: United Launch Alliance's manufacturing facility in Decatur, AL produces Centaur upper stages. Following NASA's February 2026 cancellation of the SLS Exploration Upper Stage, ULA's Centaur V was selected as the new SLS upper stage for future missions.

label: Maxar Technologies — Gateway PPE (Palo Alto, CA)
sub: Maxar Technologies' facility in Palo Alto, CA is developing the Gateway Power and Propulsion Element (PPE), which will provide electrical power and high-power electric propulsion for the Lunar Gateway station using solar electric propulsion.

label: NASA Ames — Orion Heat Shield Testing
sub: NASA Ames Research Center in Silicon Valley conducted the 121-test heat shield investigation campaign following Artemis I's unexpected charring of the Avcoat material. The Arc Jet Complex at Ames simulated the extreme reentry heating conditions to diagnose the anomaly.

label: White Sands Test Facility — Orion Abort System Tests
sub: NASA's White Sands Test Facility in New Mexico is used for Orion launch abort system testing and propulsion system qualification. The Abort Motor and Attitude Control Motor for Orion's Launch Abort System were tested here prior to Artemis I.

label: Plum Brook Station — Orion Thermal Vacuum Testing
sub: NASA Glenn Research Center's Plum Brook Station in Sandusky, OH houses the Space Power Facility — the world's largest thermal vacuum chamber — where the Orion crew module and service module underwent combined environmental testing in 2019–2020 prior to Artemis I.

label: Axiom Space — Artemis Lunar Spacesuits (Houston)
sub: Axiom Space in Houston is the sole remaining contractor for next-generation Artemis lunar spacesuits (xEMU replacement), after Collins Aerospace discontinued its competing xEVAS development. Axiom's Axiom Extravehicular Mobility Unit (AxEMU) will be used for Artemis III+ lunar surface operations.

label: Pacific Splashdown Zone — Orion Recovery
sub: The primary recovery zone for Orion capsules is in the Pacific Ocean west of Baja California. Artemis I splashed down here on December 11, 2022, recovered by the USS Portland. USS San Diego is designated as the primary recovery ship for Artemis II.

label: Northrop Grumman — SRB Testing (Promontory, UT)
sub: Northrop Grumman's Promontory, Utah facility manufactures and tests the five-segment solid rocket boosters used on SLS Block 1. Each SLS flight uses two SRBs derived from Space Shuttle technology, producing 3.6 million pounds of thrust combined.
